publisher: Springer Science and Business Media LLC
abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Income-based energy poverty metrics ignore people’s behavior patterns, particularly reducing energy consumption to limit financial stress. We investigate energy-limiting behavior in low-income households using a residential electricity consumption dataset. We first determine the outdoor temperature at which households start using cooling systems, the inflection temperature. Our relative energy poverty metric, the
                    <jats:italic>energy equity gap</jats:italic>
                    , is defined as the difference in the inflection temperatures between low and high-income groups. In our study region, we estimate the energy equity gap to be between 4.7–7.5 °F (2.6–4.2 °C). Within a sample of 4577 households, we found 86 energy-poor and 214 energy-insecure households. In contrast, the income-based energy poverty metric, energy burden (10% threshold), identified 141 households as energy-insecure. Only three households overlap between our energy equity gap and the income-based measure. Thus, the energy equity gap reveals a hidden but complementary aspect of energy poverty and insecurity.
                  </jats:p>
title: Unveiling hidden energy poverty using the energy equity gap
The energy austerity pitfall: Linking hidden energy poverty with self-restriction in household use in Austria
Wind repowering: Unveiling a hidden asset

abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
        <jats:p>Inequality is usually discussed in a monetary perspective, while the disparity in energy access sometimes is more severe. This paper mainly studies the impact of urbanization on urban-rural electricity consumption inequality with a modified STRIPAT model. We find that there is a long-run U-shaped relationship between urbanization and urban-rural electricity consumption inequality, and the estimated turning point arrives at the urbanization level of around 60%. We further carry out a regional heterogeneity analysis and then have two interesting findings: firstly, the colder northern region’s turning point arrives later than the south. Secondly, the baseline U-shaped relationship remains for developed eastern regions, while for the unde- veloped midwest, the relationship is not nonlinear but linearly neg- ative. As an extension, we lastly explore the mechanism underlying the U-shaped relationship, and find that the interaction of urbaniza- tion’s scale and efficiency effect determines the U-shaped relationship.</jats:p>
title: Urbanization and Energy Equity: An Urban-rural Gap Perspective

title: Conclusion: Overcoming Barriers to Decentralized Energy Systems in India
